description:
🔗https://www.beshak.org/advisor/?formstart=1&utm_source=Youtube&utm_campaign=REALDEALbehindNivaReassure3.0 

Not freshers or call centres – consult India’s top 5% insurance experts, powered with sophisticated research on Beshak. Book your FREE call NOW!

Welcome to the video where we discuss the real deal behind Niva Bupa Reassure 3.0 

No eyes glinting,  loaded praises about plans and brands. 

Because we know you don’t want fluffy features, you want the real deal.   

Meet the plan that’s promising to give you EVERYTHING you’ve ever wished for in health insurance – and maybe a little more! 👀

In this video, Mahavir Chopra (Founder & CEO, Beshak.org) breaks down Niva Bupa One – their latest health insurance product that might just be the most ambitious plan yet. We cover its 6 biggest features – with all the fine print you must know.

Key Highlights:
✅How the Infinite Sum Insured really works (and the hidden catch!)
✅The truth about Day 1 coverage for 145 illnesses
✅ How the Lock-the-Clock feature can freeze your premium for years
✅ Whether the international treatment cover is worth the extra cost
✅ How much value the OPD & medicine cover really gives you

Video Chapters-
0:00 Introduction & video agenda
0:48 Overview of ReAssure 3.0 plan
1:18 Highlight 1: Infinite sum insured
1:59 Highlight 2: Zero waiting period for 145 diseases
2:58 Highlight 3: Lock the clock (premium freeze)
4:52 Highlight 4: Borderless (international treatment cover)
5:42 Highlight 5: Well Consult (OPD rider)
6:56 Highlight 6: Niva Bupa One membership (executive health checkup)
7:40 Core features 
8:01 Core features explained (room rent, surgeries, treatments, waiting periods, daycare fine print)
10:35 Pricing across variants (Classic, Select, Elite, Black)
12:00 Claim performance metrics (settlement speed, ICR, complaints, rejections)
15:10 Recap before competitor comparison
16:02 Comparison with ICICI Lombard Elevate (key benefits & features)
20:38 Comparison of claim metrics (ReAssure 3.0 vs ICICI Elevate)
23:28 Premium comparison between the two plans
25:28 Final verdict & recap

💡 Watch till the end to avoid missing the fine print and make a smarter buying decision.
👇 Comment below – Would you pay extra for Day 1 coverage?
🔔  Don’t forget to like, share, and subscribe ‪@Beshak  for more expert insights!

comments.text:
Sir
I am 76 y and my wife 72 y old . I have a corporate floater policy of 5 lakhs with niva bupa health plus. It has  15% copayment and capping on hospital bed and ICU .

I want to port to HDFC Ergo Optima Secure  as individual floater policy for peace of mind. Is that possible? Can you please help me in doing that? I know that premium may be much higher. More over I have PED. 

Or  is it possible to buy a new policy?

Please answer
comments.author: @suprabhat110

comments.text:
Hello Sir, thank you for sharing the details so clearly. The good part is that HDFC ERGO Optima Secure does not have an upper entry age limit, so technically applying at 76 is still possible. However, at your age and with a pre-existing condition, underwriting will be stricter. The insurer may issue the policy with loading, exclusions, fresh waiting periods for any new health condition (if any), or in some cases may decline the proposal. The final decision always depends on medical underwriting after full disclosure. Porting from your corporate policy is possible in principle, but it is not automatic. For group health insurance, migration/portability is subject to terms and timelines. Buying a completely new policy is also an option, but that again would again be subject to underwriting and waiting periods.
Without understanding your full medical history, and overall risk profile, it would not be right to say whether you should port or stay. At this stage, the safest approach is to speak to an experienced advisor who understands insurer underwriting behaviour and can structure this properly so you don’t risk losing existing coverage. If you would like, we can connect you with a Beshak expert who can guide you through the right strategy for your situation.
comments.author: @Beshak

for your patience to explain so elaborately.

comments.text: My wife is having RA will she get insurance
comments.author: @uniqueuday77

comments.text:
Hi, thanks for reaching out. Health insurance becomes trickier when you're already diagnosed with a serious condition like Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA). Since RA is an autoimmune disease, most insurers consider it high-risk - and typically decline coverage altogether. If comprehensive plans aren’t available, you can explore substandard plans, or insurers experienced in handling higher-risk cases. comments.author: @sandipprajapati673

comments.text: Hi, thanks for sharing your experience. We understand how stressful and frustrating long claim delays can be. In many cases, insurers do ask for past treatment records to establish medical history and this can happen with any insurer. Navigating this process has become quite complex, which is why having an expert by your side right from the time of buying the policy often makes a big difference during claims. If you believe the delay or more document requests are unfair, we’d suggest escalating the matter to the insurer’s Grievance Redressal Officer. We’ve explained the exact escalation process here: https://www.beshak.org/insurance/opinion/articles/filing-insurance-related-complaints-in-india/
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: Higer ICR means bad business right ? More chances to default ? Please correct if i am wrong.
comments.author: @danielthomas5401

comments.text: Hi! Slight correction here. A higher ICR doesn’t automatically mean “bad business” or a higher chance of default. It simply means a larger portion of premiums is being paid out as claims. An ICR in a healthy range (often around 60–80%) is generally seen as sustainable. What a consistently high ICR can indicate is pressure on profitability and future pricing, which may result in premium hikes or tighter underwriting. But that is slightly different from an insurer being unable to pay claims or collapsing financially. Default risk is linked much more to the solvency ratio than to ICR. So, ICR is a signal to be read in context, not a standalone verdict.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text:
How to claim 2ht plus hospitalisation 
They are not giving discharge summary
comments.author: @TheMahis007

comments.text: Hi! For 2+ hour hospitalisation claims, the insurer needs documentary proof of admission and active treatment to process the claim. The original discharge summary, along with first and subsequent consultation papers, is one of the key medical documents required. If the hospital is not issuing a discharge summary, you can request an admission note plus a treatment summary or procedure note, duly signed by the treating doctor. It’s best to inform the insurer/TPA in advance about this and share whatever documents the hospital is willing to provide. As long as there is documented evidence of 2+ hours admission and active treatment, the claim should be evaluated. If documents are denied, escalate it formally to the insurer.
comments.author: @Beshak

The Lock the Clock feature is inbuilt in your ReAssure 3.0 Elite plan. comments.text: 13:05 - if I have to read between the lines, did you mean the claim payout of this company is low?
comments.author: @eggyolk2519

comments.text: Hey! What we meant is that the claim Incurred Ratio (ICR) being low could indicate that the company is either charging higher premiums or settling fewer claims compared to the premiums collected. It’s not a direct comment on payout fairness - just what the numbers suggest at a broad level. You can read more about how ICR works here: https://www.beshak.org/insurance/health-insurance/articles/incurred-claims-ratio/
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text:
Sir, please tell niva bupa 3.0 cover all prolonged treatement for Cancer,
(high cosr chemo, rediation charges) dialysis, Brain surgery etc?
comments.author: @MrSouravhalder

comments.text: Hi! Any treatment or surgery that takes 2 hours or more is covered under ReAssure 3.0. The plan also covers 12 modern treatments such as Immunotherapy (Monoclonal Antibody Injections) and Oral Chemotherapy. However, if a treatment takes less than the prescribed 2-hour duration, it won’t be covered. So, it might not cover all types of prolonged cancer treatments, only those that meet this condition.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: ​for prolonge cancer treatement which insurance is cover?
comments.author: @MrSouravhalder

comments.text: @MrSouravhalder  Hi! Most comprehensive health insurance plans covering modern treatments and all daycare treatments provide coverage for prolonged cancer treatments on an indemnity basis, provided all T&Cs are met. If you don’t have a history or pre-existing cancer, you can also explore critical illness or standalone cancer policies as they offer broader protection against expensive cancer care. If you want personal guidance, speak to a Beshak-verified advisor who can help you understand your options better and make a confident, informed choice. Here’s the link: https://www.beshak.org/advisor/
comments.author: @Beshak

Risk is same in both.
But Advantage is More.

comments.author: @ManishMukherjee

comments.text: Hello there, Switching from Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0 to ReAssure 3.0 can bring both benefits and trade-offs. The new plan offers exciting features but may also come with fresh underwriting. Migration isn’t always guaranteed - your age, health and past history will be evaluated before approval. The insurer can also decline the migration application, apply exclusions or impose loadings (additional premiums). To decide whether it’s the right move, it’s best to consult an expert who can help you compare costs, benefits and long-term implications while ensuring your current coverage remains protected.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: Should we buy super top up with reassure 3.0? Is it not required as it offers unlimited sum Insured?
comments.author: @healthiswealth9520

comments.text: Hi! While unlimited sum insured helps tackle medical inflation, it comes with catches like uncertain premium hikes, portability issues, downgrade challenges, and hidden sub-limits. A fixed sum insured is usually a more reliable option, and you can add a super top-up for higher coverage at an affordable price. That said, the choice finally depends on your needs, budget, and preferences. For personalised guidance, you can connect with our advisor.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: Not required Top up policy
comments.author: @ManishMukherjee

comments.text: But sir, elevate gives only one time option to unlimited claim
comments.author: @Saravsure

comments.text: Not exactly! Elevate gives you the option to choose Unlimited Sum Insured, and if you opt for this, certain add-ons like Power Booster (Super Loyalty Bonus), Infinite Care, and Inflation Protector are not applicable, since you are already covered with the maximum possible protection. However, if you do not select Unlimited Sum Insured, you can opt for the Infinite Care add-on at an extra cost, which provides the one-time benefit of covering hospitalisation or any single claim during the policy’s lifetime without limits on the annual sum insured
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: For elite plan... Only single private ac room is covered. Pl check sir
comments.author: @Saravsure

comments.text:
Hi there,

In the ReAssure 3.0 Elite plan, all room categories are covered except Deluxe / Suite, as per the policy documents and if we choose a room outside the eligible category, a co-pay (as per Annexure V of the policy) will apply on the entire claim; for the Elite variant, there is no co-pay for General Ward, Twin Sharing or any room category other than Deluxe and Suite.

For a detailed analysis, you may always connect with our expert over a call, visit www.beshak.org.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: I have 50 lakh health insurance of niva bupa reassure 2.0. they say there is lock the clock feature that means premium will not increase untill I make a claim but this year they have increased the premium from 33k to 37.4k. and I did not make a claim.they make false promises. Stay away from niva bupa. Then I ported my policy to hdfc ergo

comments.text: Namaste! Lock the Clock sirf aapke age-related premium ko lock karta hai jab tak pehla claim pay nahin hota. Iska matlab yeh nahin hai ki premium kabhi badhega hi nahin. Insurers ko premium revise karne ki ijazat hai medical inflation, hospital ke kharche badhne ya product repricing ke basis par – aur yeh IRDAI ki approval ke saath hota hai.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: @Beshak  When we port our policy to a new one, is the existing waiting period waived, or does a new waiting period apply under the new policy?”
comments.author: @mangaldeepchatterjee6866

comments.text: @mangaldeepchatterjee6866  Hi! When you port a health insurance policy, the waiting periods you’ve already completed get carried forward to the new insurer, as per IRDAI rules. However, porting involves fresh underwriting – so if there are any newly diagnosed conditions, the new insurer can apply a new waiting period for those.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: @mangaldeepchatterjee6866  You won't have to serve waiting periods again, if you've already served them in your existing plan. However, you may have to serve a waiting period for any newly diagnosed diseases.

comments.text: How can we migrate from 2.0 to 3.0
comments.author: @gireesank2097

comments.text: Hi! If you already have Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0 and want to switch to ReAssure 3.0, you’ll need to request a migration with the insurer. This can be done only at renewal (preferably at least 30 days before, subject to fresh underwriting and policy terms. As per the policy clause, the insurer may underwrite the proposal in case of migration, if you haven’t been continuously covered for 36 months. While IRDAI mandates migration, some insurers may temporarily restrict it for new launches - so it’s always best to confirm directly with the insurer before proceeding. To know more about migration, please check out this article: https://www.beshak.org/insurance/health-insurance/articles/portability-and-migration-of-health-insurance-policy/
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: 17:47 In case of Restoration, What do you mean by starts from 1st claim vs starts from 2nd claim? What is it's implications??
comments.author: @randomyoutuber-s7t

comments.text: yes i too need to understand that claim from 2nd.
comments.author: @avtarkaint

comments.text: “Starts from 1st claim” means the very first claim of the policy year itself can trigger restoration of cover once the base sum insured is used up, whereas “starts from 2nd claim” means restoration will only get triggered from the next (subsequent) claim onwards - so in that case, if your first hospitalisation exhausts the base cover, you’ll have to bear the excess cost for that hospitalization because restoration isn't activated yet.
comments.author: @Beshak

comments.text: 💋​ @Beshak
